narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw such a high risk—88% chance—of you struggling to pass the bar exam, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture it like this: the model looked at your journey so far and saw a consistent pattern that raised red flags, starting with your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school—that ended up being the biggest signal pushing toward failure, suggesting the demands of advanced coursework might have been a steep climb. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 25, which stood out as another strong pull downward, hinting at challenges with the kind of high-stakes reasoning the bar demands. Your Year 1 decile of 1 followed closely, painting a picture of early academic hurdles that didn't shift much over time. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model linked to added pressures that sometimes make the path tougher without extra safety nets. Your Native American background also factored in as a subtle but real influence toward risk, reflecting broader patterns we've seen in outcomes. Even your undergraduate GPA of 1.8 added to the weight, showing a foundation that the model worried might not fully support the rigors ahead. Choosing full-time study as a male applicant rounded out these signals, all combining to tell a story of someone facing an uphill battle against the odds. It's not about your potential or heart—it's this specific mix that made the model cautious, like spotting storm clouds on a sailor's horizon before setting out. The good news? You're in control of rewriting this story; focusing on boosting those grades and test prep could flip the script for future chances. Hang in there—you've got resilience in your corner already. With encouragement, [Your Guide]
